sentences.text: Where?
In Theresienstadt?
In-- in Oederan?
In Oederan-- Oederan, we were a transport of 300.
They were all girls, and they were from different nationalities, actually, because they were all already from Auschwitz.
We came from Birkenau.
And some were from Czechoslovakia-- many.
And some were from Hungary and from Poland, and some from Germany also.
And we had in-- in Oederan, we had two doctors.
They were doctors who started before the war.
They were, naturally.
But they had no medicine.
They had none.
They couldn't do anything if somebody was ill.
And we-- we had to work at the munition factory.
But many a time, when there wasn't enough work for everybody, everybody was standing near the door.
They wanted to get out into the fresh air-- to do any work, as long as you could get out of this prison.
And on-- on some of these occasions, I-- I was taken where we-- we had to go and push a cart.
And Oederan is a very hilly village.
It was terribly difficult, because we were really already so starved, you know, from all this period of-- from coming from '42 till '44, and in Auschwitz, and in the ghetto.
And it was very, very hard-going.
And that was really cold there in Germany.
We were in this very thin-- just in a-- in a-- in a dress.
We had no clothes.
We had no coat.
We had just a pair of shoes, which they had given us a pair of old shoes from-- from Auschwitz, which they-- the people-- and we went.
Often, we had to go in and clean up the places from these German women.
And they would have a whole loaf of bread lying in the-- in the-- in the rubbish bin.
And we were so starved, and we saw this bread lying there.
We knew we couldn't touch it, but it was so heartbreaking, because we were so starved.
And there was bread and all kinds of food lying around-- fruit, all kinds of things was just thrown away.
But anyway, we would go.
That was on the occasions when we used to help them clean.
And then, we would be taken by-- where they push a cart out to bring rolls, fresh rolls, bread for them, beer, wine, and we-- after--
For the Germans-- for the Germans.
And we would be pushing all this.
We would be doing the pushing and the work.
Of course, we never saw anything of it.
It was very hard-going, very hard-going.
No.
No.
We were in a prison, so like a prison.
And what's more, the Russians were coming nearer, and Chemnitz was the nearest town to Oederan.
Oederan was only like a village.
And there they were bombing all the time.
You could hear the bombs falling.
And they were running down with the-- with-- to the bunkers with their suitcases.
We were not allowed even to go near-- only a meter from the window.
We were-- we were all under lock and key.
So if a bomb would have fallen there, you know, the walls are not-- we couldn't do anything.
We couldn't go out.
We were under lock in a prison.
And that is-- we will stay there till 1944.
It was very hard, very hard.
So what happened on the day when you were moved to Theresienstadt?
In-- when we arrived in Theresienstadt, that was-- well, one early morning, I said already that they told us to-- to leave.
And we went in these trains.
All of the-- of us from the transport.
And we were going backwards and forwards.
And we-- we arrived in Theresienstadt eventually.
We never thought we will arrive anywhere.
And Theresienstadt is in-- hungry, and tired, and exhausted, and not knowing what's going to happen.
And eventually, we-- that was a ghetto during-- it was-- Theresienstadt was a ghetto, actually.
And now the Germans were running away.
And some of the places where the food was kept for the Germans was opened up.
And some people gave us-- they started cutting up-- they had salami.
And you couldn't eat it, because our stomach was so shrunk.
I shall never forget.
I had a piece of that salami and thought I'd die from that, because I couldn't digest it.
My stomach was-- there was hardly any stomach.
But anyway, what happened to me is that, after a few spoons of soup, I collapsed.
I had the tuberculosis.
That was in Theresienstadt.
Yes, right in the beginning.
I was already ill from the Lodz ghetto.
So this came out.
I had bleeding coming from my lungs-- terrible bleeding.
People didn't believe I will survive it, because there were occasions when people did have that and didn't survive.
I don't know.
It was just-- I don't know how I survived.
It was fate, I think.
I don't know.
It was a place where all the people just-- the water was still on.
The Germans were running away, which was one good thing-- that we knew that the war must be ending now.
But there were many, many-- there were heaps of Russians, dead Russians.
I couldn't believe it.
I have never seen in my life so many Russians.
Because they treated the Russians terrible-- starved them as well-- terrible.
And people were so ill.
They had dysentery.
All the rooms were-- were hospitals.
They were made into hospitals.
Otherwise, I don't know Theresien so much, because I was so ill.
I was lying either in this bed or in the hospital bed.
I can't really say so much about Theresien.
I suppose they had there-- our people, Jewish people, you know.
By this time, the Germans were already running away.
And they took you, of course.
They-- there were kitchens opened.
I mean, somebody must have been in charge there, you know, from the people.
And--
Yes.
I remember the day of the liberation.
Everybody was singing and dancing, but I was very ill.
I couldn't participate in the joy.
I was so happy, because I knew that we survived it.
But I could not actually, physically participate, because I was-- I was lying in-- in the hospital.
That was the real hospital, the Hohenelbe.
Before, I was in a room and on stretchers, wherever they put me.
Because there was-- this-- this hospital, I only got in quite late.
Because there was no room in that hospital, because so many illness and dysentery.
So it was terrible.
After liberation-- well, I was in the hospital.
I couldn't move around.
I couldn't do anything, anyway.
I had to get myself right.
With me, it was just a question when I got into the kindergarten with the youngsters, and then I started living, being together with them.
And slowly, slowly I was-- was, I suppose-- I wasn't healed, because you can't heal tuberculosis just like that.
But I was walking about.
I was living in a certain way.
But I wasn't-- I was ill.
I wasn't very well.
The only time when I actually-- watch-- what was to survive for me was to come to England and be sent to the sanatorium.
In the sanatorium, I was being treated.
And the rest and food and everything-- this is how I got better, was the sanatorium.
In the-- in the hospital.
He came around and all.
He was in charge of the hospital.
Or he must have been in charge, because he came around and was looking at everybody.
And [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
What is [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in English?
To--
Examine-- to examine you.
And--
I think he was exactly a Haeftling like any of us.
He was a somebody who was in the-- in the concentration camp, just like us.
But he took it upon himself to see after people who he could help.
And he probably helped others like he helped me.
No.
I don't remember names-- terrible things.
Apparently, there is a lady here.
She recognized me when there was a Hanukkah party.
And she said to me, you are Minia Munter.
She came over to me.
She said her husband was the one who put me on this list.
But I had no chance to talk to her.
I'm going to get hold of her and still talk to her, because I want to know whether it was the actual man who put me on the list or it was the doctor.
I'm not sure.
I think the doctor-- I don't know if it can be the doctor, because by now, he would have been a very old man, I think.
No.
I don't know.
The doctor--
--from-- from the Viennese doctor.
He was the one who-- he told me about going to England.
And in the end, he gave me the certificate that I needed to be able to come to England.
It was still lucky that in England, they didn't ask too many questions before I arrived.
Because once I was there, they weren't going to send me back anywhere anymore.
But if they did find out before-- tuberculosis is a terrible thing.
But thank goodness, what they did is, when I arrived, they took us to the-- to the doctor, you know, our whole group.
And we were being examined.
And when they saw that I had tuberculosis-- so they sent me to the-- to-- to-- to the sanatorium.
Yeah.
We went to Prague.
Yeah.
Well, no, we went.
We arrived in Prague.
And in Prague, we were waiting months for the planes to arrive, for the RAF planes.
Oh, we stayed in a hostel in-- it's like a hostel, like a Kinderheim, they called it-- a big house which they had taken over.
I think that the American Joint paid for it.
And they paid, also, the-- the RAF to-- to bring us over, the American Joint.
But the-- the RAF were the ones that brought us to-- to England.
We were, in our transport, 300-- 300 or 320.
But there are-- there were other transports who arrived later from different places.
But we, from Theresien, arrived 320.
There were only about 28 girls and the rest boys.
That's why I'm one of the boys.
They call me one of the boys.
Well, it was fantastic.
The thing was that the planes were very small, because they were only bomber planes.
They put-- if they put 30 children into this plane, they were full.
And one of the-- the pilots could see how cramped we are.
He took two boys and put them-- you know where the plane, right in the front has got, like, a bird here.
He put two boys in there.
They were sitting in that glass, sort of.
And me and another girl was-- sat by the window on a little bench.
And I looked down.
I said, oh, where are we?
And of course, these planes didn't go anything like you go now by plane.
They were going up and down, you know.
And we-- our stomachs were so fragile, so weak.
And most of them were sick.
But thank goodness, ours seemed to be all right until the pilot-- we were sitting in the cockpit inside, me and another girl-- he gave us a-- a bar of chocolates.
And we haven't seen chocolates in five, six years.
We hadn't seen chocolates-- when?
So it was such a great thing.
I thought, I shouldn't.
I took a piece of chocolate.
When I ate it, my stomach was going funny.
But I wasn't sick.
[LAUGHS] I was feeling very funny.
Then we arrived in Carlisle Airport.
It was dark.
It was in the evening.
And the-- the buses were waiting for-- coaches were waiting for us.
And they took us to Windermere, to the most beautiful place in-- in England.
We arrived there.
And there were a lot of people who were helping with this, waiting there at Carlisle Airport, which was a military airport.
And they brought us in Windermere to a enormous place, which was, during the war, for the Air Force, the barracks of the Air Force, where they lived, the Air Force.
So each one got a one-room.
We-- I had a room to myself, suddenly.
There was a window.
It had a little cupboard.
It had no clothes, but it had a little cupboard for clothes.
And it had a-- an iron bed, a little carpet, and then, of course, we were brought in to be-- and we were pampered.
They took me, and they washed my hair.
I remember they washed my hair with soap.
It was-- it was Pearl soap-- [LAUGHS] --like today, whatever.
And they gave me a bath.
And they-- I was so cozy and nice.
And then I went to bed.
It was lovely.
And the next day-- the next day we went-- we had an enormous dining room.
But the dining room was only for 150 people.
So we-- they would-- took it in two sessions.
And I remember we went for the first session into the dining room, and they brought these big lorries with bread.
But the bread was so big.
I've never seen such big bread, you know.
Anyway, white bread-- and they didn't have machinery.
They were cutting it by hand.
So they couldn't cut-- even how many people-- they couldn't cut as many pieces as we needed to eat.
And the boys, they were hiding the bread.
As soon as it came on the table, they put it in here somewhere.
The bread was gone.
And they're shouting, more bread!
More bread!
Then the food arrived.
More food!
Oh, they were so thin.
Well, you can see from the picture, from that main picture.
We were all very hungry people.
And I was in Windermere the longest with this other girl, Rosarela, and some other boys, because we were sent to the sanatorium.
The others were all sent to different hostels.
The girls were sent to Manchester.
They could study.
They could do.
But we were sent-- had-- first of all, we had to get well.
And whilst I was in Windermere, one day, I received a letter which had a picture of my sister.
It was strange, because there was my sister and two men.
And I-- I didn't know who they were.
I couldn't read it, because it was in English.
Well, apparently, what I found out is my sister was married-- got married.
And the man who was looking for family was a pilot, a British pilot who came from Leeds, which is not so far from Windermere, Lake District.
And he was looking for me.
And he found me on the list.
He found a Munter on the list, but he didn't know that I would be the sister.
My sister never believed it will be me.
So she thought it's a cousin.
And on the very first letter-- and she found out that this is her youngest sister.
There was great joy.
And my brother-in-law, who is-- was a wonderful man, really, he wrote me such a wonderful letter that now they found me.
And they-- they want now to find his brother.
He wants to find his younger brother.
And then we would be a family together.
But of course, his brother didn't survive.
[SIGHS] Yes.
We didn't know exactly, but we were very afraid.
When they took so many people with small children and ill people, that wasn't a good sign.
That was very bad.
Young people, they needed for work.
There were all sorts of young people, but that meant because they didn't want so many people.
Because they wanted to destroy us all.
Why are we survivors all together?
Because they still needed us.
They needed us to do their work.
Otherwise, there would be no survivors.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]
I couldn't be.
Once--
--I was sent to the ghetto, there was no way you could find anything out.
But the rumors were always there.
And it's really amazing.
But my mother had a premonition.
And she always used to tell me everything.
When I walked with my mother, I felt so cozy and so good.
And my mother said to me, you know, Miniale, I dreamt last night that there are some black vans coming for us.
I don't know what it meant-- whether she heard something, or there were some rumors.
But this is what she said to me, and this is exactly what happened.
Because my parents were not sent to Auschwitz.
They were sent in these vans in the woods.
I don't even know how it was, whether they were still alive or half alive or what when they-- when they buried them.
I don't know.
Well, because we heard about this afterwards.
You hear about these things afterwards.
This-- was no sign of them.
If somebody is alive, you get a sign, whatever.
You find them if they're alive.
They were not alive.
No.
They were not sent to Auschwitz, not from there.
No.
There were sent in this place-- I don't know whether it is near the places.
I'm still trying to find out one day where from Warta.
The goyim probably would know they are being-- where they were buried.
But I never went back to Poland.
I have never gone back.
But one day, I must find out for myself to know exactly where the place is-- where they are buried.
Of course, they would be buried all together in one communal-- yeah.
Well, when I got the-- he went back and told them that there is a Munter alive.
And they got in touch with me.
And I told them that, yes, I am Minia, the youngest.
Oh, I got-- we got in touch with the pilot.
He couldn't come anymore.
And then I told him that I am the young-- I am Minia Munter, the youngest.
My-- my sister couldn't believe it.
And this is when she wrote me that first letter-- and my brother-in-law.
And then we started writing, of course.
Yeah.
Well, she-- as a matter of fact, would I have gone with my sister, I would have definitely not survived.
Because she was sent to Bergen-Belsen.
And she had a very hard time in Bergen-Belsen.
And me, having been so ill-- I don't know-- just this fate.
I was sent back to Birkenau with all the people who were not good for work.
And my sister never believed I'd be alive.
And yet, I survived Auschwitz.
And my sister was sent to Bergen-Belsen.
In Bergen-Belsen, I would have never survived-- impossible.
Anyway, my sister was also very ill when she came out from Bergen-Belsen.
She has frozen hands and frozen feet.
And-- terrible.
But she was still-- she was also still young.
So she's older than me, but she was young.
And then afterwards, she met her husband, and she got married.
It's this really amazing story.
As I said before, we've come from religious people.
And my sister, when she came out of Bergen-Belsen, she and another couple of girls went to stay in-- in Luneberg, which is the nearest place from Bergen-Belsen.
And they-- they took a room together, and they stayed in this room.
But Friday night-- Friday, my sister had to light candles.
That was, for her, very important.
So she lit candles.
In the meantime, my-- my brother-in-law-- olov ha-sholom-- because he died a few years ago now.
He had a fiance before the war who he wanted very much to find.
He loved his fiance very much.
And he was going from one end of Germany to-- to-- to the other end-- from the American zone to the English zone-- to find his fiance.
In the end, he could see he-- it was-- there was no sign of her.
And he went past this point, he had to pass this side little street in-- in Germany, in Luneberg and sees candles, the light.
So he knocked at the door.
And my sister had just washed her hair, and she was wearing a scarf, was careless-- she put-- didn't know she made her hair.
It looks-- the hair was already grown.
It was already quite longish by this time.
And when he came in, and they started talking-- and they got to like each other.
Anyway, he-- they decided-- he was older than her.
And she was at an age where she could marry easily.
So this is what-- when they got around, they started-- they decided to get married.
My brother-in-law is a-- was a very intelligent man.
During the war, in the ghetto, when everybody was running here, running there, he also had to do all his work, whatever.
But when he was trying to hide at night or whatever-- in the morning-- he had a violin, because he lived in the Lodz before the war.
And he taught himself violin in a cellar, someone overture.
Or he would teach himself English.
Hebrew-- he was a teacher of Hebrew.
He was teaching everybody before they came to-- to-- to-- from Germany to Luneburg.
In Luneburg, he was like a organizer.
Pesach, he did the seder for everybody, because he is a Mizrahi.
He was a religious man.
And he also-- he-- he taught-- taught Hebrew for people who wanted to go to Israel.
And because he knew a little bit of English, because he taught in there a little bit of English, the-- the Red Cross-- because Luneburg is the British zone.
And they got married still when they were in-- in-- in Bergen-Belsen.
So the Red Cross gave everybody a parcel who got married.
And my brother got two parcels, because he could speak English.
They liked him so much.
They got married.
And then, of course, they had the children there, two children.
But my brother-in-law wanted nothing but to go to Israel.
I mean, he could have gone to America in those days from Luneburg or wherever.
But no.
He only wanted Israel.
He was a very great Zionist.
And he worked very, very hard in Israel.
He used to get up at 4 o'clock in the morning.
He had a little bit of a rest in the afternoon, and then till 11 o'clock at night every day, he worked, except Shabbat, you know.
But he worked very, very hard.
And they-- they built themselves a house.
And he had a-- a-- like a-- if you call it-- a supermarket, like a shop, a village shop, you know.
They had that.
And he really worked very, very hard.
I could tell you so many things about him, because he was such a wonderful man.
He even-- when he didn't like something with the politics in Israel-- he would write into the newspaper.
And he would get replies.
But he was very clever, my brother-in-law.
That was in Kfar HaRoeh, near Hadera.
It's not far from Netanya, either.
Yeah.
We were-- in Windermere, we were like, you know, just as you-- you imagine-- taking people out of a prison, and suddenly they are free.
We were enjoying every day, every moment.
Windermere has got a-- a-- was a lake.
So we used to go down.
Sometimes we were treated to a boat trip.
Or we used to go for walks.
I shall never forget.
We went once-- without-- we had [NON-ENGLISH], you know, that-- that looked after us.
And once, she took us to a shop there near-- not far from Windermere-- another town.
And a lot of girls came in.
And I could see that they were buying buttons.
But I couldn't pick up one word.
I didn't know one word of English.
I could hear them, but I couldn't pick-- it was really amazing.
English is such a difficult language to learn, you know.
You don't realize, because the spelling, and the writing, and the reading-- it's terribly difficult for a foreigner.
And to still to say T-H is-- is-- I still can't do it.
[LAUGHS] It's very difficult.
And I've been here now many years-- since 1945.
So the activities was mainly, you know, we were meeting, and going for walks, and having meals.
I mean, for us, that was an important item, to get a little bit of the strength back and everything.
Yes.
It was lovely.
It was really paradise.
It was-- Windermere was for us, paradise, real paradise.
I'm sorry to interrupt.
We have to change tapes again.
